title: Meeting Targets in Competitive Product Markets
abstract: We show that public banks face negative stock return jumps after missing their earnings per share (EPS) targets, and theoretically and quantitatively link these jumps to bunching behavior in the EPS surprise distribution. Bunching banks cut deposit rates to meet their targets, but do so at the expense of deposit outflows and franchise value losses. Local competitors, including private banks unexposed to capital market pressure, increase deposit rates, compensating depositors for switching. Our results provide new evidence that performance targeting incentives can affect consumer product prices, and suggest that competition may provide a check on public firms' targeting efforts.

article.introduction:
Performance targeting is an economicallyrelevant and pervasive phenomenon in modern financial markets. In recent years, performance targeting and its prejudicial counterpart “quarterly capitalism” (Barton (2011)) have gained attention in industry, policy, and academic circles. The general consensus is that, by encouraging managers to sacrifice investment in long-term value in favor of short-term outcomes, performance targeting has negative welfare consequences. Prior theoretical and empirical work highlights the distortions that performance targeting can have on firm investment in physical, intangible, and human capital (e.g., Almeida et al. (2020a,2020b)), and quantifies sizable welfare losses arising from these distortions (Terry (2023)).
  Like investment, product pricing is a critical strategic decision undertaken by firms. However, while survey evidence shows that pricing is a common margin of adjustment for corporate managers seeking to meet performance targets (Graham, Harvey, and Rajgopal (2005)), the academic literature has paid surprisingly little attention to the effects of performance targets on pricing. Importantly, these pricing decisions may interact with the industrial organization of the sector and thus have implications for competitors and consumers.
  In this paper, we conduct the first systematic study of how public firms' incentives to meet performance targets affect their product market behavior, using retail deposits of U.S. commercial banks as a laboratory.1We start our analysis by connecting two sets of empirical facts on short-term performance targeting in the banking industry. In line with prior work focusing on nonfinancial firms (e.g., Bartov, Givoly, and Hayn (2002), Bhojraj et al. (2009), and Bird, Karolyi, and Ruchti (2019)), we show that banks' announcement returns feature a significant negative jump (a notch) around earnings announcements conditional on missing the earnings per share (EPS) target. Using bunching techniques (Saez (2010), Kleven and Waseem (2013)), we provide robust evidence of an abnormal spike in the percentage EPS surprise distribution precisely at zero, and of missing mass in the interval [−2.5%, 0) of the distribution. These findings show that banks that would just-miss the target absent the return notch manage their EPS to exactly meet the target.
  We build a simple structural model to connect the two observed patterns in announcement return notches and EPS surprise bunching. In the model, managers face EPS consensus estimates, have private information about the true “latent” EPS of the bank (Guttman, Kadan, and Kandel (2006)), and can engage in earnings management to increase reported EPS (possibly at the expense of long-term value). As we show, realized EPS surprises cleanly map into announcement returns, which increases earnings management incentives when managers' wealth is tied to the bank's stock. However, earnings management is privately costly, which limits managerial incentives to manipulate. We show that a negative return notch for just-missing the EPS consensus target distorts earnings management incentives. Moreover, consistent with the data, a negative return notch creates an abnormal spike in the realized percentage EPS surprise distribution relative to a counterfactual in which announcement return notches are absent.
  The structural model also allows us to uncover primitive parameters underlying earnings management incentives. Using the indifference condition of marginal bunchers (i.e., banks indifferent between increasing costly earnings management to exactly meet their EPS targets and facing return notches for missing the target), we estimate an elasticity of earnings management to its stock market–based benefits (in terms of higher announcement returns) in the 2.94 to 3.26 range. Our estimates imply a quantitatively meaningful relationship between announcement returns and earnings management incentives.
  Our model and quantitative estimates suggest that banks make adjustments to avoid negative return notches upon missing their EPS targets. In the remainder of the paper, we examine how banks may use a previously understudied tool, namely, deposit rate-setting, to achieve these targets. To do so, we construct a bank-quarterIn Spikeindicator that is equal to one for banks falling in the estimated spike range (and likely to have engaged in earnings management) and zero otherwise, and we link theIn Spikeindicator to quarterly average deposit interest rates at the bank-deposit product-county level.2
  We show that public banks in the EPS surprise spike range have significantly lower deposit rates than control banks outside the spike range: EPS targeting is associated with a 3% reduction in the average product rate in our sample. This estimate is quantitatively similar when we condition on restrictive fixed effects, indicative of within-bank dynamic adjustments across deposit products, and is stable across a battery of robustness tests. For example, the results are quantitatively similar when we limit attention to control group observations above or below the zero-EPS-surprise threshold, and when we include several sets of covariates, including covariates related to other earnings management strategies such as abnormal loan loss provisions (LLPs) (Beatty and Liao (2014)). In contrast, our results completely disappear in placebo tests using banks just outside the spike range, which suggests that our results are unique to banks within the spike range and helps rule out alternative explanations based on EPS values rather than on the distance between realized and target EPS.
  We provide several additional tests to validate the proposed targeting mechanism. First, we document similar effects at the bank level using total interest expense normalized by total deposits as the dependent variable, suggesting that banks adjust rates on their existing callable deposit base in a similar way as they adjust local deposit rate quotes, which we observe with more granularity. Second, we show that aggregate interest rates pose a natural limit on banks' ability to meet targets by deposit rate-setting. Targeting banks' rate-cutting behavior is more pronounced when aggregate interest rates are high and for short-term, inelastic products such as checking and savings. However, aggregate rates provide an upper bound on deposit rates (Drechsler, Savov, and Schnabl (2017)), and the extent to which banks can cut rates is lower when aggregate rates are low. In these cases, rate-cutting only affects deposit products with long maturity, not short-maturity products with rates close to zero.
  Third, we show that the main effect does not vary as a function of bank characteristics that reflect the use of other tools to achieve the target, suggesting that rate-cutting is a complement to rather than a substitute for other targeting tools. Fourth, when we study rate dynamics around targeting quarters in a stacked difference-in-differences framework (Cengiz et al. (2019)), we find no evidence of preexisting deposit rate trends between targeting and nontargeting banks before the targeting quarter. Targeting banks start cutting rates in the first month of the quarter, and quickly revert to the original values at the beginning of the following quarter.3
  Having documented that public banks cut deposit rates when they face targeting pressure, we show that these rate cuts are associated with deposit outflows. Using bank holding company (BHC) data from the Federal Reserve Call Reports, we document a 1.16% decline in targeting banks' total deposits relative to the sample average, and declines of similar magnitudes in interest-bearing deposits. Consistent with these changes being driven by targeting banks' rate-cutting, we observe no changes in non–interest-bearing deposits. In stacked difference-in-differences regressions, we show that deposit outflows persist for around two quarters after the end of the targeting quarter. These outflows are quantitatively larger for time deposits, which have relatively high rate elasticity, than for callable deposits, which have relatively low rate elasticity.
  Overall, we show that despite deposit outflows, rate cuts do indeed allow banks to achieve their quarterly targets—the counterfactual distribution of EPS surprises if targeting banks did not cut their rates and face the associated deposit outflows features more mass in the bunching range just below zero than the actual distribution. At the same time, deposit outflows for three quarters are associated with an average decline of 0.11% in the equity market-to-book of the average bank in our sample (Egan, Lewellen, and Sunderam (2022)). Compared to the average return notch of −0.5% upon missing the target, this estimate suggests that the benefits of real earnings management via deposit rate-setting exceeds the long-run costs associated with deposit outflows.
  We next turn to the aggregate effects of targeting-induced rate-cutting at the local level and examine how banks respond when their local competitors cut rates to meet short-term targets. To do so, we implement a novel estimation method designed to measure within-market spillover effects (Berg, Reisinger, and Streitz (2021)). In our specific setting, we use this method to estimate the effect of targeting banks' incentives on other local banks with targeting incentives as well as on public and private banks without such incentives (i.e., on control banks and private banks).
  We find that targeting banks' rate-cutting is unaffected by competing banks' behavior, suggesting that performance targets act as binding constraints on individual firms' pricing. At the same time, public and private local competitorsincreasetheir deposit rates in response to local competitors' targeting incentives. We show that these rate hikes are consistent with competitors' effort to compensate depositors for switching and search costs (Yankov (2023)) and thus to attract a wider deposit customer base (Gourio and Rudanko (2014), Gilchrist et al. (2017)). Taken together, our aggregate analysis suggests that competition provides a natural balancing force for the transmission of targeting incentives to consumer prices. Our results complement the theoretical work of Klemperer (1987) and Dasgupta and Titman (1998), who argue that in the presence of switching costs, an increase in product prices (or in our case a decrease in deposit rates) can boost short-term profits but lead to long-lasting market share losses.
  Our paper adds to two strands of literature. Following the seminal work of Stein (1989), a long literature explores the causes and consequences of managerial short-termism. Prior work highlights several factors that can help explain short-term incentives, including listing status (Falato and Scharfstein (2016)) and features of managerial contracts such as stock-based compensation (Benmelech, Kandel, and Veronesi (2010)).4Another branch of this literature highlights performance targets as a source of short-term incentives. This branch documents positive earnings announcement returns for firms that just-beat their targets (e.g., Bartov, Givoly, and Hayn (2002), Bhojraj et al. (2009)), and links this pattern to managerial targeting incentives (Guttman, Kadan, and Kandel (2006), Bird, Karolyi, and Ruchti (2019)). Another branch of this literature seeks to shed light on the tools used by managers to meet their targets. These tools include changes in accounting practices, as well as changes in real activity (Graham, Harvey, and Rajgopal (2005)). Many of these papers focus on cutting current period expenditures, including research and development (R&D) expenses (Roychowdhury (2006), Almeida et al. (2020b), and Terry (2023)), advertising expenses (Cohen, Mashruwala, and Zach (2010), Chapman and Steenburgh (2011)), and capital investment (Almeida et al. (2020a)).5
  We contribute to this literature in two ways. First, we use techniques from the bunching literature in public economics (Saez (2010), Kleven and Waseem (2013)) to detect abnormal bunching in the EPS surprise distribution, and to theoretically and quantitatively link negative return notches upon missing EPS targets to managerial incentives to manipulate earnings. We contribute a new application of bunching methods to estimate the elasticity of earnings management to its stock market–based benefits, and, in doing so, provide a new methodology to detect earnings management incentives that complements existing reduced-form approaches (e.g., Dechow, Sloan, and Sweeney (1995) and Dechow et al. (2012)). Second, we document that banks cut deposit rates, a primary component of interest expense, when they face benchmark-driven performance incentives (i.e., when they bunch). To the best of our knowledge, this is the first direct evidence on the effects of performance targeting on consumer prices since the influential survey by Graham, Harvey, and Rajgopal (2005).6
  This paper also complements the banking literature on the supply-side determinants of deposit rates (Ben-David, Palvia, and Spatt (2017)). We confirm previous results on market structure and deposit rate flexibility (e.g., Diebold and Sharpe (1990), Hannan and Berger (1991), Hannan (1994), Driscoll and Judson (2013), Yankov (2023)), and we explore the implications of capital market pressure on deposit interest rates for individual banks, competitors, and the local economy. Our paper also contributes to the financial intermediation literature on competition in local deposit markets. The literature has long recognized deposit competition as an important economic force (e.g., Berger and Hannan (1989,1997), Neumark and Sharpe (1992)) that drives bank business models (Drechsler, Savov, and Schnabl (2021)), bank value (Egan, Lewellen, and Sunderam (2022)), and monetary policy transmission (Drechsler, Savov, and Schnabl (2017)). However, direct evidence on how local competitors respond to changes in other banks' deposit pricing strategies is scarce, perhaps due to the dearth of external variation in individual banks' deposit rates or to only recently addressed challenges in estimating competitive spillover effects (Berg, Reisinger, and Streitz (2021)). To the best of our knowledge, our analysis provides the first estimates of how one bank's rate-setting incentives affect local competitors, and how rate-setting incentives and competitive responses vary and aggregate as functions of local market characteristics.
  The rest of the paper is organized as follows. In SectionI, we describe the data. In SectionII, we document negative announcement return jumps for banks that just-miss their EPS targets, as well as bunching at zero in the EPS surprise distribution. In SectionIII, we link these two patterns through the lens of a bunching model, and we recover key model parameters using structural estimation. In SectionIV, we show that banks cut deposit rates to achieve their EPS targets, and we study the associated trade-offs and competitive responses. SectionVconcludes.
article.I. Data:
Our data come from three main sources. We obtain quarterly realized and forecasted EPS values for each BHC in our sample from the Institutional Brokers Estimates System (I/B/E/S). We restrict the sample to realized and forecasted EPS values for listed BHCs with deposit rate data available on RateWatch, and we follow Terry (2023) by adjusting EPS for stock splits.7In SectionIof theInternet Appendix, we provide novel stylized facts on the timing of EPS forecast releases and analyst consensus updates in the year before the release of banks' actual EPS, and we describe how we use the data to guide us in the definition of the target.8Based on the evidence reported in that section, we define the EPS target for banki$i$in quartert$t$,y¯it$\overline{{y}}_{it}$, as the median EPS forecast across all analysts' most recent forecasts in the[−30,−10]$[-30,-10]$weeks from the actual EPS release date. Consistent with previous literature (e.g., Terry (2023)), this definition allows sufficient time for managers to adjust their actions to EPS targets while also removing stale estimates from the data. As shown in FigureIA.1, forecasts from early in the quarter are relevant to consensus formation, so we adjust the definition of the target to include these forecasts.9
  Our second main data source is RateWatch. We use these data to obtain weekly annualized percentage yields (APYs) quotes at the bank-branch level on different deposit products. We restrict the sample to the eight most common deposit products available in the data, namely, MMAs of at least $25,000, savings accounts of at least $2,500, interest-bearing checking accounts of at least $2,500, and CDs with maturities of 3, 6, 12, 24, and 36 months of at least $10,000. These eight products comprise about 40% of all quotes in the RateWatch database in any given year, and reflect substantial heterogeneity in maturity and other contractual features. In our bank-level tests, we focus on within-county variation in performance targeting incentives for different banks, so we aggregate within-quarter variation while maintaining geographic heterogeneity by collapsing the APY observations for each product to the bank-county-quarter level.
  Our third main data source is the consolidated BHC Call Report data from the U.S. Federal Reserve. We use quarterly BHC data to construct measures of average interest rates paid on different types of deposits, to study deposit outflows for targeting banks, and to conduct robustness and covariate balance tests.
  Our main sample comprises a panel of 521,545 bank-product-county-quarter observations for the period from the first quarter of 2003 to the last quarter of 2018. In TableI, we present summary statistics for the main dependent variables and covariates in the paper. The first row of TableIreports summary statistics for our main EPS surprise measure from equation (1). We find that the average EPS surprise in our sample is 1.41%, with a standard deviation of 7.34% and a median of 1.28%. The following three rows report summary statistics for deposit rates, both unconditionally and by product maturity. The average deposit rate in our sample is 0.85%, with a standard deviation of 1.18% and a median of 0.25%. The data further reveal large variability in rates across maturity, with short-term products (i.e., checking, savings, MMAs and three-month CDs) offering an average rate of 0.4% (standard deviation 0.7%) and longer maturity products (i.e., CDs with maturity exceeding one quarter) offering an average rate of 1.28% (standard deviation 1.38%). In the remaining rows of TableI, we report summary statistics for the balance sheet variables used in the paper, collapsed at the BHC-quarter level.
  In our tests, we use data from several secondary sources, including the Center for Research in Security Prices (CRSP) and the Wharton Research Data Services (WRDS) Event Study Suite, the Federal Deposit Insurance Corporation (FDIC) Call Reports and Summary of Deposits (SOD), the Community Reinvestment Act (CRA) lending data, data on brokerage closures and mergers and acquisitions (M&As) manually updated following Hong and Kacperczyk (2010), Compustat, Execucomp, and Dealscan. We provide more details on how we use these and other secondary sources in the relevant sections of the text and in theInternet Appendix.
article.II. Announcement Return Jumps and Bunchi:
Surveys of CFOs suggest that executives are willing to alter real economic decisions in order to meet quarterly performance targets (Graham, Harvey, and Rajgopal (2005)). Large-sample observational studies corroborate this survey evidence by showing that an abnormal mass of firms tend to just-meet their performance targets, and those that do also cut discretionary expenses, including R&D expenditures and investment in capital and labor (e.g., Roychowdhury (2006), Almeida, Fos, and Kronlund (2016), and Terry (2023)). Consistent with early theoretical work on the sources of target-based incentives (Guttman, Kadan, and Kandel (2006)), empiricists document a significant jump in stock prices around earnings announcements conditional on firms meeting their performance targets (e.g., Bartov, Givoly, and Hayn (2002), Bhojraj et al. (2009), and Bird, Karolyi, and Ruchti (2019)). According to this evidence, the discontinuous jump in stock prices around quarterly earnings targets provides executives with stock-based compensation incentives to meet their targets.
  We extend prior work focused on the nonfinancial sector to commercial banks. In Figure1, we document large jumps in the distribution of realized buy-and-hold abnormal returns (BHARs) in a five-day window around banks' earnings announcements. In Panel A, we report average market reactions in 80 equally spaced bins of the EPS surprise distribution, as well as estimates and 95% confidence intervals derived from local polynomial regressions fitted on each side of the zero-EPS-surprise threshold. In Panel B, we restrict the EPS surprise window to a narrower[−5%,5%]$[-5\%, 5\%]$interval around the zero-EPS-surprise threshold. In both panels, the data show a statistically significant and negative jump in five-day BHARs of around 58 basis points (bps) for banks that just-miss their EPS targets. As previously documented for nonfinancial firms (Bushee (2001), Matsumoto (2002)), investors with a short-term orientation seem to penalize banks for failing to meet their EPS targets.
  In Figure2, we document bunching in the EPS surprise distribution just above the threshold level of zero. In Panel A, we report a histogram of banks' EPS surprises in the[−20%,20%]$[-20\%, 20\%]$window around the zero-EPS-surprise threshold. The data show a large spike in the EPS surprise density at zero. Approximately 15% of the EPS surprise observations in this interval are clustered exactly at zero.
  We use techniques originally developed in the public economics literature (Saez (2010), Kleven and Waseem (2013), Kleven (2016)) to quantify the extent of bunching in the EPS surprise distribution. We use these methods to estimate thespike rangeof abnormal observations just above the zero-EPS-surprise threshold for which we can conjecture earnings manipulation, and thebunching rangeto the left of the zero-EPS-surprise threshold in which manipulators' EPS surprises would lie in a counterfactual world without manipulation incentives. We denote the estimated spike range byŷLow,ŷHigh$\left[\hat{y}_{{Low}}, \hat{y}_{\text{High}}\right]$and the estimated bunching range by0−Δŷ,0$\left[0-\Delta \hat{y},0\right)$.
  To jointly estimate the spike range and the bunching range, we follow Kleven and Waseem (2013) and Alvero and Xiao (2020). We first group EPS surprise observations intoJ$J$equally spaced bins,[y∼j−1,y∼j]j=1,2,⋯,J$\left\lbrace [\tilde{y}_{j-1},\tilde{y}_j]\right\rbrace _{j=1,2,\dots,J}$, and compute the empirical density functionĥ(j)=nj/d$\hat{h}(j)=n_j/d$in each bin, wherenj$n_j$is the fraction of total EPS surprise observations falling in binj$j$, andd$d$is the bin size. Second, we exclude EPS surprise observations around zero, fit a polynomialĥ0$\hat{h}_0$on the nonexcluded range of the distribution, and interpolate the polynomial over the entire support. Third, we determine the spike range of EPS surprise observations for which the empirical distribution exceeds the fitted counterfactual distribution in a narrow window around the threshold. Formally, we letŷLow=maxy∼j−1<0y∼j−1$\hat{y}_{\text{Low}}=\max _{\tilde{y}_{j-1}<0} \tilde{y}_{j-1}$such thatĥ0(j)≥ĥ(j)$\hat{h}_0(j) \ge \hat{h}(j)$, andŷHigh=miny∼j>0y∼j$\hat{y}_{\text{High}}=\min _{\tilde{y}_{j}>0} \tilde{y}_{j}$such thatĥ0(j)≥ĥ(j)$\hat{h}_0(j) \ge \hat{h}(j)$.
  Panel B of Figure2provides a visual illustration of how we jointly determine the spike range and the bunching range. The figure plots the empirical distribution of EPS surprises (ĥ$\hat{h}$, solid black line), as well as its estimated counterfactual distribution (ĥ0$\hat{h}_0$, solid maroon line) in the same [−20%, 20%] EPS surprise range used in Panel A. For illustrative purposes, we use a relatively large bin sized=0.5%$d=0.5\%$and a polynomial of orderk=7$k=7$estimated outside an excluded range of[−6%,1%]$[-6\%, 1\%]$and interpolated over the entire support. Panel B shows clear evidence of a mass spike at the zero-EPS-surprise threshold, such that our estimated lower and upper bounds for the spike range (dashed vertical lines in black) are exactly equal to the lower and upper bounds of the two bins just above and just below the threshold. Formally, we find thatŷLow=−0.5%$\hat{y}_{\text{Low}}=-0.5\%$(i.e., the upper bound of the bin closest to but just below the threshold) andŷHigh=0.5%$\hat{y}_{\text{High}}= 0.5\%$(i.e., the lower bound of the bin closest to but just above the threshold). Panel B also shows the bunching range lower bound (−Δŷ$-\Delta \hat{y}$, vertical dashed line in maroon), which we obtain from equation (3).
  In TableII, we tabulate the bunching estimation results for different parameter choices. In Panel A, we report point estimates and semiparametrically bootstrapped standard errors for the lower bound of the spike rangeŷLow$\hat{y}_{\text{Low}}$, the upper bound of the spike rangeŷHigh$\hat{y}_{\text{High}}$, and the lower bound of the bunching range−Δŷ$-\Delta \hat{y}$as we vary the orderk$k$of the fitted counterfactual polynomialĥ0$\hat{h}_0$while keeping the bin size of the realized distributionĥ$\hat{h}$fixed tod=0.1$d=0.1$bps. In Panel B, we report estimates and standard errors for the same quantities as we vary the bin sized$d$of the realized distribution while keeping the counterfactual polynomial size fixed tok=7$k=7$. In both panels, the last column corresponds to the benchmark specification that we use in the rest of the paper. Standard errors are bootstrapped with 10,000 replications using the semiparametric method described in SectionIIIof theInternet Appendix(see, for example, Kleven and Waseem (2013)).
  The first two rows of Panels A and B in TableIIconfirm that the spike range is exactly located at the zero-EPS-surprise threshold: Independent of the counterfactual polynomial order and bin size, the upper and lower bounds of the spike range are equal to the lower bound of the bin closest to but just above the threshold and the upper bound of the bin closest to but just below the threshold, respectively. The bootstrapping exercise described in SectionIIIof theInternet Appendixshows that our estimates are statistically significant at conventional levels, with associatedt$t$-statistics in excess of three under most polynomial and bandwidth specifications. The third row of Panels A and B also provides consistent estimates of the lower bound of the bunching range. TableIIshows that this lower bound varies from −2.26% to −2.52%, depending on the polynomial and bin size choices. These estimates are statistically significant at conventional levels. InInternet AppendixTableIA.XX, we also show that our results, particularly the upper and lower bound estimates of the spike range, are robust to alternative choices for the excluded range used to fit the counterfactual distributionĥ0$\hat{h}_0$.11
  Overall, TablesIIandIA.XXprovide strong quantitative evidence of bunching at the zero-EPS-surprise threshold, and Figure1suggests that banks bunch to avoid negative return jumps associated with missing their EPS targets. In the next section, we link these two patterns through the lens of a bunching model.
article.III. Conceptual Framework and Structural:
We build a simple structural model to study stock market–based manipulation incentives to meet earnings targets. We show that discontinuous jumps in returns around earnings announcements give managers an incentive to manipulate earnings, and illustrate how the resulting manipulation generates a spike in the EPS surprise distribution just above zero. We estimate the structural model to recover parameters governing the elasticity of earnings manipulation to stock market–based incentives and the long-term losses from manipulation.
  We model earnings management as a trade-off between the stock market–based benefits of reporting high quarterly earnings and the manager's private costs of making within-quarter adjustments to firm policies (Guttman, Kadan, and Kandel (2006) and Bird, Karolyi, and Ruchti (2019)). While in this paper we focus on one specific adjustment that has received little attention in the literature, namely, consumer product pricing, our framework encompasses both accounting-based earnings management (Dichev et al. (2013)) and other forms of real earnings management (e.g., Roychowdhury (2006), Almeida et al. (2020a,2020b)) for both banks and noninancial firms.12
  Consensus, Latent, and Reported Earnings. Time is infinite and discrete. Within each time period (a quarter, denoted byt$t$), the following events happen in sequence. At the beginning of the quarter, each bank manager faces a vectory¯t=y¯t+1,y¯t+2,⋯,y¯t+N$\mathbf {\bar{y}}_t = \left\lbrace \bar{y}_{t+1}, \bar{y}_{t+2}, \dots, \bar{y}_{t+N} \right\rbrace$that describes the consensus projections about her bank's future after-tax profits per share (i.e., projected EPS) for up toN$N$quarters ahead. These projections are unbiased estimates for every period (current and future). At the beginning of the quarter, the manager also privately learns a vector oflatentEPS projections,yt∗=yt+1∗,yt+2∗,⋯,yt+N∗$\mathbf {y}^{\mathbf {*}}_t = \left\lbrace y^*_{t+1}, y^*_{t+2}, \dots, y^*_{t+N}\right\rbrace$for up toN$N$quarters ahead. We assume that for allt$t$andn$n$, raw latent EPS surprisesyt+n∗−y¯t+n$y^*_{t+n} - \bar{y}_{t+n}$and percentage latent EPS surprisesyt+n∗/y¯t+n−1$y^*_{t+n}/\bar{y}_{t+n} - 1$are generated from mean-zero smooth distributions. Additionally, for allt$t$,y¯t,yt∗>0$\mathbf {\bar{y}}_t, \mathbf {y}^{\mathbf {*}}_t>\mathbf {0}$.
  After observing consensus and latent EPS projections, the manager can take a nonnegative earnings management action during the quarter,qt≥0$q_t \ge 0$. This earnings management action increases current-quarter private EPS toyt+1=yt+1∗+qt$y_{t+1} = y^*_{t+1} + q_t$, and possibly decreases long-term private projected EPS toyt+n=yt+n∗−λ∼qt$y_{t+n} = y^*_{t+n} - \tilde{\lambda } q_t$for all1<n≤N$1<n\le N$, whereλ∼≥0$\tilde{\lambda } \ge 0$is a parameter governing long-term value losses.13As a result of earnings management, the private EPS vector then becomesyt=yt+1∗+qt,yt+2∗−λ∼qt,⋯,yt+N∗−λ∼qt$ \mathbf {y}_t = \left\lbrace y^*_{t+1} + q_t, y^*_{t+2} - \tilde{\lambda } q_t, \dots, y^*_{t+N} - \tilde{\lambda } q_t \right\rbrace$. At the end of the quarter, the vectoryt$\mathbf {y}_t$is reported and is incorporated into the next quarter's consensus EPS projectiony¯t+1$\mathbf {\bar{y}}_{t+1}$.14
  Since, as we show below, earnings announcement returns are functions of true EPS surprises but not EPS levels, the assumption that latent EPS surprises are ex ante mean zero in each quarter allows us to treat quarters as independent and to study the effect of current earnings management on announcement returns without dependence on past earnings management.15As a result, we remove time-dependency throughout.
  Stock Prices around Earnings Announcements. Marginal investors price bank stocks as the present value of a dividend stream and incorporate the most recently available EPS information. Before public EPS announcements, the most recently available information is incorporated in the consensus vector,y¯$\mathbf {\bar{y}}$. After reported EPS is made public, the most recently available information is incorporated in the reported vectory$\mathbf {y}$.
  The discontinuity in returns around the zero-EPS-surprise threshold generates a subset of banks whose managers find it optimal to increase earnings managementq∼$\tilde{q}$relative to the counterfactual scenario described in the previous paragraphs, leading to an abnormal spike in the observed EPS surprise distribution. Two types of banks are included in this spike range: (i) banks that would report zero EPS surprise in the counterfactual scenario and continue to report zero when facing the discontinuous function (10) and (ii) banks that would report less than zero EPS surprise in the counterfactual but report zero when returns are given by (10). The latter banks are included in the interval−Δy∼,0$\left[-\Delta \tilde{y},0\right)$in the counterfactual scenario.
  Themarginal buncheris the bank that would report EPS surprises equal to−Δy∼$-\Delta \tilde{y}$in the counterfactual and is indifferent between reporting zero and its interior solution−Δy∼′$-\Delta \tilde{y}^{\prime }$under the discontinuous return function (10). In SectionIVof theInternet Appendix, we follow the public economics literature (see Saez (2010), Kleven and Waseem (2013)) and use the marginal buncher's indifference condition, as well as estimates of the return notches for missing the target,ξ$\xi$, the bunching range,−Δy∼$-\Delta \tilde{y}$, and the cost of capital,r$r$, to recover elasticity estimatesε̂$\hat{\varepsilon }$in the 2.94 to 3.26 range. Intuitively, suppose that the benefit of earnings management in terms of higher announcement returns (i.e.,δ$\delta$) increases by 1%. This could be due to an increase in the discount rate,r$r$, a decrease in the long-run loss from earnings management,λ$\lambda$, or both. Then, holding the current-period consensusy¯$\bar{y}$fixed, our estimates imply that we should expect earnings management (i.e., the difference between reported and latent EPS) to increase by around 3%.
  Our baseline bunching estimation confirms a quantitatively meaningful relationship between announcement returns and earnings management incentives. In SectionIVof theInternet Appendix, we complement these estimates with estimates of the long-run loss parameterλ$\lambda$. Using cross-sectional variation in bunching incentives and the cost of capital across banks with different characteristics (similar to Di Gregorio and Paradisi (2021)), we recover an upper bound for the present value of long-run losses,λ̂$\hat{\lambda }$, of around 87.9%. In other words, our estimates imply that a 1% increase in earnings managementq$q$has a positive net present value benefit of 12.1 bps, on average. These additional tests therefore confirm long-run distortions from earnings management, but suggest that these distortions are relatively small and that earnings management can be value-increasing from an ex ante perspective.
article.IV. Pricing Deposits to Meet Targets:
We build on the evidence from the previous sections to ask whether likely manipulators in the spike range behave differently from other banks. We focus on one previously unstudied policy, namely, the deposit interest rate setting, which has direct implications for consumers and deposit market competitors.
  Facing pressure to meet their EPS targets, bank managers may consider different tools to increase revenue or cut costs in the income statement. In this paper, we focus on the trade-off between cutting interest expenses by decreasing deposit rates and facing long-term deposit franchise losses (Klemperer (1987), Dasgupta and Titman (1998)). While we do not argue that bank managers rely solely on deposit rate-setting while ignoring other potential tools to achieve targets, there are at least three institutional reasons that may make deposit rate-setting a relatively attractive targeting tool for bank managers.
  First, decreasing rates on a relatively inelastic deposit base can lead to substantial cost savings, and thus boost short-term EPS—deposits constitute about 80% of total liabilities for publicly listed commercial banks in the United States, and deposit interest payments constitute around 40% of total expenses for these banks (Egan, Lewellen, and Sunderam (2022)). Additionally, bank profits are highly sensitive to deposit interest expenses. Holding fixed the quantity of deposits, a one-percentage-point decrease in the deposit interest expense ratio would decrease total expenses by over 15% (Egan, Lewellen, and Sunderam (2022)). In SectionIV.D, we use a simple income statement equation to formalize how small changes in deposit rates allow banks to cut costs and just-meet their EPS targets in our setting.
  Second, our theoretical framework suggests that banks should quickly reverse any adjustments made to increase the likelihood of meeting EPS targets. Short-term adjustments are easier to implement and reverse using short-term deposit rate-setting as opposed to other tools such as loan pricing or investment.22Third, the amortization of interest expenses over short maturities may also contribute to the relative attractiveness of adjusting deposit rates to meet short-term earnings targets.23
  We do not argue that bank managers rely solely on deposit rate-setting while ignoring other potential adjustments to achieve targets. For example, the literature shows that banks under short-term earnings pressure use syndicated loan fees and covenant enforcement to boost revenue (Bird et al. (2022), Ertan (2022)), and they reduce LLPs to cut costs (Beatty and Liao (2014)). Alternatively, managers may choose to modify their loan pricing strategies to boost interest income. In theInternet Appendix, we investigate whether managers use these alternative adjustments as complements or substitutes for deposit rate-setting by presenting covariate balance tests and robustness tests that incrementally include these tools as control variables. Our estimates are stable across specifications, consistent with deposit rate-setting being used independently of the alternatives for performance targeting.
  Deposits also offer various measurement advantages to study the effects of targeting on consumer product prices, which may be challenging in other contexts. First, deposit rate data are available at a weekly frequency and with fine geographic granularity, which allows us to study the relationship between performance targeting and deposit pricing across and within banks, over time, and in different competitive environments. Second, banks generally offer deposits directly to customers, which allows us to study the pricing effects of performance targeting without worrying about possible pricing distortions along the supply chain (Clark (1995), Nakamura and Steinsson (2008)). Third, pricing is one of the most important dimensions over which banks compete in deposit markets, which arguably reduces confounding variation arising from brand loyalty that we would expect to be more relevant in nonfinancial consumer products.24At the same time, banks offer a variety of standard deposit products that vary in maturity and in the extent to which they offer transaction services. This cross-product variation leads to different degrees of rate elasticity, which allows us to formulate predictions on which products' quantities may be more sensitive to pricing decisions.
  In TableIII, we show that performance targeting is associated with deposit rate cuts. Our baseline specification in column (1) includes bank, quarter type, product, and county-quarter fixed effects separately, isolating differences in rates between targeting and nontargeting banks operating in the same county and quarter while controlling for average rate differences across banks and products (Evans and Kessides (1993,1994)). Column (1) shows that EPS targeting leads, on average, to 2.5 bps lower interest rates across all deposit products in our sample, which is around 2.9% of the sample mean or around 2.1% of a standard deviation.
  In the following columns of TableIII, we progressively saturate the baseline specification with bank-quarter type fixed effects to control for differential rate-setting behavior by the same bank across isolated and nonisolated targeting quarters, product-county fixed effects to control for time-invariant characteristics of the local deposit market, and bank-product(-county) fixed effects to further control for bank specialization in different product markets (in the same county). The results in columns (2) to (5) remain economically and statistically unchanged across specifications, reducing concerns that the rate-setting results may be driven by these characteristics.
  In column (4), we present results of a fully saturated specification with bank-quarter type-product-county fixed effects and product-county-quarter fixed effects. This specification allows us to focus on variation in rates for the same product offered by the same bank in the same county over time while holding county-level economic conditions fixed. Despite the inclusion of the most restrictive set of fixed effects available for our panel, the results of column (4) confirm that public banks significantly reduce their deposit rates when they face targeting incentives.
  We perform multiple robustness tests and additional analyses to validate our main result and targeting mechanism. Most of the analysis relative to this section is relegated to SectionVof theInternet Appendix.
  Robustness on Baseline Result. TableIVshows that our results are quantitatively similar when we drop control group observations below the zero-EPS-surprise threshold, thus comparing targeting banks to those that exceed their targets, or above the zero-EPS-surprise threshold, thus comparing targeting banks to those that miss their targets. Additionally, the results disappear completely when we remove spike range observations from the data and estimate “donut” specifications (similar in spirit to Dowd (2021)) to detect rate discontinuities above and below the zero-EPS-surprise threshold. The results also disappear when we compare banks above and below the zero-EPS-surprise thresholdbut not in the spike rangeto other banks. Taken together, the results in TableIVconfirm that the rate-setting behavior of banks around earnings targets is unique to banks in the spike range. Because the absolute value of EPS varies bothacrossthe EPS surprise distribution andwithinbanks in the spike range, these results also help rule out alternative explanations related to changes in the absolute value of EPS rather than to the relative distance between EPS and its target.26In SectionVof theInternet Appendix, we conduct a number of additional robustness tests on our baseline results.
  Mechanism Validation. In TableV, we show that reductions in deposit ratequotesat the local level are also accompanied by significant drops in deposit interestexpenseat the bank level. In the first two columns of TableV, we show that depending on the panel structure, targeting banks have 3.3 bps to 3.7 bps lower deposit interest expense to total (lagged) deposits. These effects are quantitatively similar to the main effects we find in interest rate quotes. For example, the average (annualized) quarterly deposit interest expense to total lagged deposits in our sample is around 116 bps, such that the effect documented in column (1) of TableVcorresponds to a 2.8% decrease relative to the sample mean. Since the average repricing maturity of bank liabilities is a little over a quarter (Drechsler, Savov, and Schnabl (2021)), these magnitudes suggest that targeting banks reduce deposit rates on theirexistingcallable deposit bases as they do for newly issued deposits.
  In columns (3) and (4) of TableV, we further study how deposit rate quote changes are quantitatively related to total (normalized) deposit interest expense. In column (3), we show that a one-percentage-point increase in average product APY quotes (around 85% of a standard deviation) at the product-county level is associated with a 0.61 percentage point increase in the deposit expense ratio at the bank level in the same quarter (around 60% of a standard deviation), consistent with the short repricing maturity of deposits. In column (4), we regress the predicted values from column (3), that is, changes in deposit expense ratios driven by changes in deposit rate quotes, on theIn Spikeindicator. Consistent with the results of TableIIIand columns (1) and (2) of TableV, we find that the component of normalized interest expense predicted by variation in local quotes is around 2.5% lower for targeting banks than for nontargeting banks.27
  Next, in TableVI, we show that the aggregate interest rate poses a natural limit on banks' ability to meet targets by deposit rate-setting, thus complementing the evidence in Drechsler, Savov, and Schnabl (2017). In the first two columns, we show that the baseline deposit rate cuts documented in TableIIIrevert almost completely in periods of expansionary monetary policy, confirming that banks have less room to adjust rates downward when their levels are already low. In the remainder of the table, we document heterogeneity in the main and reversal effects based on product maturity. Our estimates suggest that relative rate cuts outside of periods of expansionary monetary policy are larger for short-term products such as savings and checking than for longer-maturity products such as 6- and 12-month CDs. For example, columns (3) and (5) of Panel B show 2.7 and 6.2 bp baseline rate reductions for short- and long-maturity products, respectively, which translate to 27.0% and 10.3% reductions relative to the unconditional sample medians in short- and long-maturity products. This result is consistent with Yankov (2023) and Egan, Lewellen, and Sunderam (2022), among others, who show that short-maturity products have lower demand elasticity than long-maturity products. In other words, the samerelativerate reduction should lead to lower outflows in short-maturity products than in long-maturity products, and thus justify largerrelativerate cuts in short-maturity products during targeting quarters.
  Additionally, short-term products, which have rates close to zero when aggregate interest rates are low, experience a full reversal of the main effect in periods of expansionary monetary policy. In contrast, the reversals are quantitatively smaller for long-maturity products, suggesting that banks may depend more on these products for EPS targeting in times of expansionary monetary policy. Overall, the results of columns (4) to (6) confirm the intuition that the shorter maturity and lower elasticity of short-maturity products make them better-suited than other products for short-term targeting adjustments. However, banks may need to rely on longer-term, relatively elastic products when aggregate rates are low because the spread on short-term products is not large enough for a cut to materially affect net income. In SectionVof theInternet Appendix, we conduct three additional sets of tests to further validate the proposed mechanism.
  In Figure3, we plot the coefficientsβ2$\beta _2$and their associated 95% standard errors, using event-monthMofE=−3${MofE} = - 3$as the baseline. In column (1) of TableIA.XXII, we formally tabulate the results of Figure3. Our estimates show that targeting banks cut the average rates on their deposit products by 1.8 bps at the beginning of the quarter, relative to event-monthMofE=−3${MofE} = - 3$and relative to control banks unaffected by targeting incentives. These rate cuts are economically small (less than one-third of the within-targeting quarter cuts), statistically insignificant before the start of the targeting quarter, and persistent only for one month after the end of the targeting quarter. Overall, Figure3and column (1) of TableIA.XXIIprovide evidence consistent with targeting incentives driving banks' rate-cutting behavior, and show that such rate-cutting is short-lived after the targeting quarter. In SectionVof theInternet Appendix, we provide additional evidence on these dynamic incentives.
  In columns (2) and (3) of TableIA.XXII, we study differences in rate dynamics across products with different maturities. These columns highlight three sets of results. First, the observed rate cuts' dynamics are qualitatively similar across deposit products: The data document larger rate cuts at the beginning of the targeting quarter and a quick reversal after the targeting quarter ends. Second, consistent with TableVI, short-maturity (and relatively inelastic) products experience relativelylargerrate cuts than long-maturity products, relative to both the sample mean and, in particular, the sample median. For example, rate cuts in the third month of the targeting quarter amount to around 9.0% of the unconditional median for short-term products, and to only 4.3% of the unconditional median for long-term products. Third, short-maturity products' rate cuts are statistically significant at conventional levels only in the last month of the targeting quarter. In contrast, long-maturity products exhibit rate cuts for around four months, starting at the beginning of the targeting quarter. This evidence suggests that long maturities and depositors' liquidity considerations require that banks act relatively early to meet performance targets using time deposits, while the short-term and relatively inelastic nature of other products such as savings and checking accounts allow banks to use these products for short-term adjustments toward the end of the targeting quarter.
  Having established that banks reduce deposit rates when they face targeting pressure, we examine whether these rate changes are associated with quantity responses and if so whether the quantity responses depend on product rate elasticity.
  To do so, in TableVII, we start by reporting our estimates of the coefficientβ$\beta$in equation (13), which allows us to study whether and how performance targeting is associated with deposit outflows. In column (1), we show that targeting is associated with a 0.9% reduction in domestic deposits to total assets, corresponding to a 1.16% reduction relative to the sample mean and a 7.5% reduction relative to the sample standard deviation.30The remaining columns of TableVIIprovide evidence consistent with these quantity changes being driven by deposit rate cuts around targeting events. In column (2), we find a larger 1.2% reduction in normalized interest-bearing deposits, corresponding to a 1.95% reduction relative to the sample mean and a 9.9% reduction relative to the sample standard deviation. In contrast, the data show no statistically significant difference between the noninterest bearing deposits of targeting banks and those of control banks.
  Our second set of tests show that short-maturity products such as savings and checking accounts experience smaller outflows in targeting quarters. Consistent with Driscoll and Judson (2013) and Egan, Lewellen, and Sunderam (2022), we find evidence that long-maturity products have larger demand elasticity than short-maturity products.32InInternet AppendixTableIA.XXIV, we then show that callable deposits experience quantitatively smaller and statistically weaker outflows than time deposits around targeting quarters.33Callable deposits to total assets experience 1.1% to 1.4% reductions in the three quarters following the targeting quarter, corresponding to 1.4% to 2.5% of the sample mean. In contrast, time deposits to total assets experience 0.5% drops during the targeting quarter, corresponding to around 4% of the sample mean. Overall, the results in TablesIA.XXIIIandIA.XXIVprovide evidence suggesting that the deposit changes documented in the main TableVIIare indeed driven by product-level rate changes in targeting quarters.
  To conclude our bank-level analysis, we ask whether rate-cutting does indeed allow public banks to meet their EPS targets, and we provide back-of the envelope estimates of the long-run losses associated with deposit outflows.
  In Figure4, Panel A, we plot the distribution of estimated EPS changes (in USD cents) for banks in the spike range. The figure shows that, even net of deposit outflows, rate cuts allow banks to increase their EPS, and around 70% of these EPS changes are smaller than 1 cent. However, cutting rates allows some banks to increase their realized EPS by as much as 16 cents (around one-third of the sample EPS standard deviation). Panel B further shows that these EPS changes allow banks to target their benchmarks by superimposing the counterfactual EPS distribution conditional on EPS changes on the actual EPS distribution. In this exercise, only targeting banks (i.e., banks in the spike range) move their position in the counterfactual distribution, while the position of other banks remains unchanged. Panel B shows that almost all of the targeting observations in the actual EPS surprise distribution are reallocated below the zero surprise threshold, and that the majority of these observations are reallocated within the bunching range−2.5%,0$\left[-2.5\%, 0\right)$estimated in TableII. While the back-of-the-envelope exercise of Figure4relies on somewhat restrictive assumptions such as effect homogeneity and constant tax rates across banks, the exercise suggests that even small observed rate adjustments allow banks to bunch and meet their EPS targets.
  We report the results of our baseline estimation exercise in TableVIII. The first row of column (1) confirms that short-term performance targeting is associated with a 3.4 bp reduction in average rates across all deposit products. This reduction is equivalent to around 3.6% of the average rate in the sample of listed banks and is not statistically different from the baseline effect in TableIII. The second and third rows of column (1) report our estimates of the coefficientsβ2$\beta _2$andβ3$\beta _3$in equation (19), which, respectively, pin down rate spillovers on other targeting banks and on nontargeting banks in the same county and quarter.
  The second row of column (1) documents a small and statistically insignificant spillover effect on other targeting banks, implying that targeting banks do not change their rate-setting behavior based on other banks' behavior.37In contrast, the third row shows that banks without short-term targeting pressureincreasetheir deposit rates in response to other banks' targeting incentives. The magnitudes are large: If 10% of the banks operating in a county face targeting incentives (and thereby reduce rates by 3.4 bps on average), the remaining 90% increase rates by around 0.95 bps on average. In column (2), we also confirm that the results of column (1) are stable as we include incrementally more restrictive fixed effects to the specification.
  In columns (3) and (4) of TableVIII, we split the baseline estimates from the first two columns and separately examine targeting spillovers on nontargeting public banks and on private banks. Our results show competitive responses that are nearly twice as large for public banks than for private banks, and complement the literature on bank ownership structure and performance (Saunders, Strock, and Travlos (1990), Laeven and Levine (2009), Falato and Scharfstein (2016), Garel, Martín-Flores, and Petit-Romec (2020)) by showing that private and public banks compete differently in local deposit markets. Our findings also complement Sharma (2021) by illustrating that banks' ownership structure matters for product market behavior. Since public banks are, on average, larger than private banks, our results also complement the literature on loan market segmentation by small and large banks (Berger et al. (2005), Berger and Black (2011)) with new evidence from the liability side of the balance sheet.
  We next study the economic sources of rate hikes by local (nontargeting) competitors. If depositors' decisions were determined solely by interest rates, absent any frictions, competitors would not need to increase their rates to attract targeting banks' depositors—if anything, reduced competition would allow competitors to reduce rates. However, in the presence of switching and search costs (Klemperer (1987)), nontargeting banks might find it optimal to increase their deposit rates to attract a wider customer base (as in Gourio and Rudanko (2014) and Gilchrist et al. (2017)). In TableIX, we study spillover heterogeneity based on characteristics of the local deposit markets (branch distance and deposit market structure, columns (1) and (2)) and of the banks operating in local markets (deposit fees, employee productivity as proxied by average salary, and local presence, columns (3) to (5)) that may affect depositors' utility and switching propensity.
  In the first column of TableIX, we start by interacting the baseline spillover effect indicator with the average distance between bank branches in a county to test the hypothesis that the documented rate hikes reflect an effort to compensate depositors' switching costs and information asymmetries (Yankov (2023)). Following the literature on distance in lending, column (1) rests on the intuition that it is more costly for depositors to physically reach the bank (Degryse and Ongena (2005), Herpfer, Mjøs, and Schmidt (2023)) and gather information (Agarwal and Hauswald (2010), Bellucci, Borisov, and Zazzaro (2013)) as distance increases. The results in this column support the switching costs hypothesis. In particular, controlling for distance reduces the baseline spillover effect documented in TableVIII: In a hypothetical county in which the average distance between branches is 0 km, rates would increase by only 0.66 bps if 10% of the local banks had targeting incentives.38However, a 10-km increase in the average distance between bank branches in the county is associated with a 2.5 bps increase in the baseline effect. At the average branch distance in our sample (14 km), the estimated coefficient is therefore 53% larger than the baseline effect of 6.6 bps.
  In the second column of TableIX, we examine whether spillovers also depend on the competitiveness of local deposit markets by interacting the spillover indicator with an indicator equal to one if the county's deposit Herfindahl-Hirschman Index (HHI) is above the unconditional sample median, and to zero otherwise. We find stronger spillovers in less-concentrated markets. While the baseline spillover estimates remain significant and have similar magnitudes as those in TableVIII, the incremental interaction effect for high-HHI counties is negative, partially offsetting the baseline effect. This result provides a first piece of evidence suggesting that local market structure may limit the transmission of individual banks' targeting incentives to consumers.
  In columns (3) to (5) of TableIX, we study local banks' characteristics that may affect investors' utility and switching propensity. We find quantitatively larger rate spillovers (suggestive of lower marginal switching propensity) when the average bank operating in the county charges relatively low deposit fees, when its employees are more productive, and when the average bank has a more local deposit footprint. These results complement those in Drechsler, Savov, and Schnabl (2017) by suggesting features of local deposit markets that may contribute to market structure and competition.
  In SectionVof theInternet Appendix, we provide two sets of additional results. First, we use the estimates of TableVIIIto study how public banks' targeting incentives aggregate up as functions of local market structure. We show that when only a few targeting banks lower their deposit rates, targeting does not have a large effect on average rates in the local economy, and there is little room for competitive spillovers to mitigate these negative effects. In contrast, when many local banks are targeting, targeting has a large negative effect on average rates, and competitive spillovers have a larger offsetting effect on average rates. Second, we provide evidence suggesting that nontargeting banks' rate hikes do indeed allow them to capture part of targeting banks' deposit outflows, and that this results in higher profitability from core banking activities. Overall, these results show that product market competition may provide a check on firms' ability to alter their pricing strategies to meet short-term performance targets, and they suggest that studies of the impact of performance targeting on aggregate outcomes should account for competitors' incentives.
article.V. Conclusion:
In this paper, we use the laboratory of consumer deposit products to study how public firms adjust product prices to meet their short-term performance targets. We show that public banks face negative return jumps upon missing their EPS targets, and we theoretically and quantitatively link these return jumps to bunching just above zero in the EPS surprise distribution. When bunching, public banks cut deposit interest rates, thus reducing their largest source of expenditure. However, while rate-cutting allows banks to meet their EPS targets, it also leads to deposit outflows and associated losses in franchise value. Using new spillover estimation techniques, we show that local competitors, including purely private banks that are not directly exposed to capital market pressure, respond by increasing deposit rates to compensate for depositors' switching costs and ultimately benefit from deposit inflows.
  Although the availability of granular data across markets and standardized products in the banking sector support the internal validity of our findings, the extent to which our findings generalize to other sectors and to other potential targeting incentives is unclear and warrants further investigation. For example, as Graham, Harvey, and Rajgopal (2005) point out, targeting firms may have incentives to reduce retail prices and increase volumes, which points in the direction of heterogeneity across sectors, consumer products, and services. Our findings also suggest that competitive forces may balance the pressure to meet performance targets through changes in other corporate policies, such as investments in capital and innovation (e.g., Terry (2023)). In our view, whether performance targeting incentives disrupt corporate policies in other industries and the extent to which they spill over to impact unaffected firms are important questions for future research.
  Editors: Stefan Nagel, Philip Bond, Amit Seru, and Wei Xiong
